title: The noting practice
subtitle: A short sitting meditation for observing thoughts without following them.
excerpt: A five-minute meditation from Day 14 of the SLEEP program. The noting practice asks you to silently label each thought as it arises (thinking, planning, worrying) and then return to the breath. The label creates a small gap between the thought and the response.
sourceNotes: The noting practice is a traditional mindfulness technique, widely attributed to Mahasi Sayadaw's Burmese Vipassana tradition (c. 1940s) and popularised in Western contexts by teachers including Tara Brach and Joseph Goldstein. Used here as a companion practice for Day 14 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive to Fall Asleep Faster and Wake Energised (Rebecca J Page, 2025).

content.text: A five-minute sitting meditation from Day 14 of the SLEEP program. The noting practice gives thoughts a label and a place to go, which turns them from urgent directives into passing events. The skill built here transfers directly to the 3am loop.
Sit comfortably with your eyes closed or gaze soft. Set a five-minute timer.
Bring your attention to the breath. Not controlling it, just noticing the rise and fall.
When a thought appears (and it will), give it a one-word label. "Thinking." "Planning." "Worrying." "Remembering." The label doesn't need to be precise. Any word that names the category is enough.
After labelling, return to the breath. The thought may continue or dissolve. It doesn't matter. Your only job is to notice, label, and return.
Repeat for the full five minutes. There is no goal other than the noticing itself. The mind will wander many times. Each return is the practice.

The noting practice is a traditional mindfulness technique from the Vipassana tradition, widely taught in Western contexts. Used here as a companion to Day 14 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive to Fall Asleep Faster and Wake Energised (Rebecca J Page, 2025).
